unitTitle: KS1 Y1 Digital painting (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: How can we paint using computers?
lessons.objectives:
I can name some paint tools
I can use a tool to make marks on the screen
I can say what a tool does
I can draw a picture using the paint tools
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: paint program · tool · paintbrush · pencil · erase · fill · undo. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the paint program (paintz.app or Microsoft Paint) open on each screen. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is a paint program? On the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE what a paint program is for, then look at a tool icon and CHOOSE its name.
   • Support: name 1 tool.  • Core: name 2 tools.  • Challenge: name the tool from its job.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — the tools. Teacher names paintbrush, pencil, fill, eraser, undo and models the pencil. Likely error: “the fill went everywhere” → fix-words “close the shape, then undo and try again.”
3. (20–30) Be a tool detective. Pupils try each tool and watch what it does, then MATCH each tool to its job on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: match 3 tools.  • Core: say what fill and undo do.  • Challenge: say which tool fixes a wrong line and why.
4. (30–50) You-do — draw a face. Pupils draw a picture of their face using the tools and screenshot it onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows one tool they used.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Using shapes and lines
lessons.objectives:
I can find the square tool and the line tool
I can use the shape and line tools to make a picture
I can fill shapes with colour
I can use undo to fix a mistake
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: square tool · line tool · fill tool · undo tool · primary colours · Piet Mondrian. TA: paint program open for everyone. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — on the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE the primary colours (red, yellow, blue) and pick the tool for a line and for a box.
   • Support: pick the colour set.  • Core: pick line and square tools.  • Challenge: explain why undo helps.
2. (10–20) I-do — the artist Piet Mondrian. Show a Mondrian picture (link from the original deck — copyright, not embedded). Name the tools: square, line, fill, undo. Likely error: line not straight → fix-words “drag slowly, or undo and try again.”
3. (20–30) We-do — ORDER the steps to make a Mondrian picture on the activity worksheet (square → lines → fill → leave some white).
4. (30–50) You-do — make a Mondrian picture, fill boxes with red/yellow/blue, screenshot onto the activity worksheet. Challenge: multi-select what is true about a Mondrian. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows one box they filled.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Making careful choices
lessons.objectives:
I can choose the right shape for the job
I can choose colours for my picture
I can build a picture from shapes
I can make a picture in the style of an artist
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: shape tool · fill tool · Henri Matisse · collage. TA: paint program open for everyone. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — the artist Henri Matisse (photo on the starter worksheet). Pupils CHOOSE how Matisse made his pictures (cutting out shapes — a collage) and pick the shape tool.
   • Support: pick how he made it.  • Core: name the shape tool.  • Challenge: name two tools that fix a wrong colour.
2. (10–20) I-do — show “The Snail” by Matisse (link from the original deck — copyright, not embedded). Bright shapes that spiral. Which tools? shape, fill, undo (not spray can).
3. (20–30) We-do — SORT good choices from bad choices for a shape picture on the activity worksheet.
4. (30–50) You-do — build a picture from bright shapes, fill them, screenshot onto the worksheet. Likely error: shape filled wrong colour → fix-words “undo, pick the colour, fill again.” TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows a favourite shape.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Why did I choose that?
lessons.objectives:
I can say what each paint tool does
I can choose the best tool for the job
I can pick colours for my picture
I can say which tools helped me
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: tool · choose · colour · feeling · Wassily Kandinsky. TA: paint program open for everyone. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — on the starter worksheet pupils MATCH each tool to its job (paintbrush, fill, eraser, undo) and choose the best tool to colour a whole circle.
   • Support: match 2 tools.  • Core: match all 4.  • Challenge: choose the best tool for a job.
2. (10–20) I-do — quick-fire: hold up the question, pupils vote A/B/C for what each tool does. Likely error: using the eraser to change a colour → fix-words “to change a colour use undo or fill, not the eraser.”
3. (20–30) The artist Wassily Kandinsky — show “Squares with Concentric Circles” (link from the original deck — copyright, not embedded). Choose colours for a feeling.
4. (30–50) You-do — make circles inside squares, choosing the best tool each time and saying why, then RATE how easy the tools were (slider) and screenshot onto the worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil names one tool that helped and why.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Painting all by myself
lessons.objectives:
I can make dots of colour on the page
I can change the brush colour
I can change the brush size
I can paint a picture on my own
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: Georges Seurat · pointillism · dots · brush size. TA: paint program open for everyone. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud. Today is an independent task.
1. (0–10) Starter — on the starter worksheet pupils CHOOSE what pointillism means (dots of colour) and the best tool for dots (paintbrush).
   • Support: choose what pointillism is.  • Core: choose the dot tool.  • Challenge: explain when to use small dots.
2. (10–20) I-do — the artist Georges Seurat (link from the original deck — copyright, not embedded). Model making dots. Likely error: dragging makes a line → fix-words “tap, do not drag.” Then ORDER the steps for a dotty sunflower on the activity worksheet.
3. (20–30) We-do — change the brush colour and brush size; bigger dots are quicker, smaller dots add detail.
4. (30–50) You-do (independent) — paint a dotty sunflower on your own, RATE how independent you were (slider), screenshot onto the worksheet. TA: help with saving only.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows their dots.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Comparing computer art and painting
lessons.objectives:
I can name different ways to make a picture
I can spot how a picture was made
I can say what is different about computer and paper
I can say which way I prefer
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: computer · paper · paint · like · prefer. TA: paint sets, paper, aprons ready for the practical task; the pupils’ sunflowers from lesson 5 to hand. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — spot how a picture was made (chalk photo on the starter worksheet); CHOOSE that neat, block-colour lines mean a computer made it.
   • Support: spot the chalk picture.  • Core: spot the computer picture.  • Challenge: give a reason.
2. (10–20) I-do — lots of ways to make a picture: chalk, crayon, felt tip, paint, computer. Look for the clues on each.
3. (20–40) You-do (practical) — paint a sunflower on paper with real paint, like last lesson but on paper. Movement break before painting.
4. (40–50) Compare — SORT facts into painting on a computer vs on paper, RATE which they preferred (sliders), and screenshot/photo their two sunflowers side by side. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; class vote: computer or paper, with a reason.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
